us-politics.headline: US Jets Downed Over Iran; Congress Pushes Back on War
us-politics.body:
Two US warplanes were shot down during operations in Iran, with at least one crew member still missing as recovery efforts continue. Two US helicopters were also hit during the search, raising the human and military cost of the conflict sharply. The Pentagon has not confirmed full details of the incidents.

On Capitol Hill, Republican Senator John Curtis called for ending offensive operations without a formal declaration of war, and Rep. Nancy Mace voiced concern about escalation. A Democrat slammed prediction market Polymarket for taking bets on the missing pilot's fate. The war is fracturing Republican unity, with some members demanding Congress assert its constitutional war powers before operations go further.

sources.title: What to know about US fighter jets hit in Iran war
2 US helicopters hit during recovery efforts in Iran
GOP senator calls to end Iran operations without formal declaration of war
Two U.S. warplanes shot down; search ongoing in Iran for 1 missing crew member
Mace ‘concerned about escalation’ in Iran war
Democrat rips Polymarket for taking bets on fate of US pilot shot down over Iran

us-politics.headline: Trump Reshapes DOJ, Cabinet Amid Hegseth Military Fallout
us-politics.body:
Todd Blanche, Trump's former personal attorney, has taken over as the top official at the Justice Department following Pam Bondi's departure. Blanche told reporters there is no evidence in the Epstein files that Epstein worked as a spy. Trump is weighing further Cabinet changes but wants to avoid what aides describe as a massive shake-up.

Separately, Republicans in Congress praised Army Chief of Staff General George after Defense Secretary Pete Hegseth ousted him, a rare public break with the Pentagon chief. Senator Thom Tillis set a firm condition on any attorney general nominee, saying support for the January 6 rioters is a red line. FBI Director Kash Patel and General McCormick warned that foreign terror threats inside the US grew during the Biden administration.

sources.title: Trump weighs more Cabinet changes, but wants to avoid ‘massive shake-up’
Todd Blanche takes on DOJ’s top job and the burden of Trump’s expectations
Blanche: No evidence in Epstein files he was a spy
Tillis sets Jan. 6 red line on attorney general nominee
GOP praise pours in for Army chief of staff ousted by Hegseth
Patel, McCormick warn foreign terror threats inside US grew during Biden years

us-politics.headline: Trump Orders, Lawsuits, and Budget Fights Pile Up at Home
us-politics.body:
Trump signed an executive order targeting college sports reform, directing changes to athlete compensation and governance rules. Oregon's attorney general sued the administration over a separate Trump order restricting vote-by-mail, calling it a source of confusion and distrust. Colorado lawyers reported that the state's court e-filing system now requires them to certify they will not assist ICE.

On the budget, Trump proposed a $10 billion fund for Washington DC construction and beautification while cutting funding elsewhere. Republican Senator Susan Collins publicly criticised the White House budget proposal over its funding cuts. Senate Democrats pressed the Treasury Department over its decision to add Trump's name to US currency. Trump also directed DHS to pay employees during a partial government shutdown.

sources.title: Trump directs DHS to pay employees during shutdown
Trump signs order aimed at reforming college sports
Trump again orders changes to ‘save’ college sports
‘Confusion, chaos and distrust’: Oregon challenges Trump’s order restricting vote-by-mail
Susan Collins knocks funding cuts in White House budget proposal
What to know about Trump’s defense budget proposal
Colorado lawyers say court e-file system now makes them certify they won’t assist ICE
Senate Democrats press Treasury Department on decision to add Trump name to currency
Trump proposes $10B fund for D.C. construction, beautification projects

us-politics.headline: Retirement Savings Erode as Trump Marks Good Friday
us-politics.body:
A new report from a major payroll firm shows Americans are saving less for retirement, with contribution rates and participation declining across income levels. Analysts warn the trend could deepen financial insecurity for future retirees if it persists through periods of economic uncertainty.

On a lighter note, Trump issued a Good Friday message saying America needs God and pointing to what he called a resurgence of religion in the country. He also gave rare public praise to a persistent Republican critic following a White House vote on a ballroom project. A Nebraska Senate candidate restructured their campaign after a complaint over payments made to family members.

sources.title: Warning sign? Retirement saving habits eroding, payroll firm says
Trump says ‘America needs God’ in Good Friday message touting ‘resurgence of religion'
Trump gives rare praise to persistent GOP foe after White House ballroom vote
Nebraska Senate candidate restructures campaign after complaint over payments to family: report

au-politics.headline: Pezzullo Found 'Reckless' in Liberal Powerbroker Dealings
au-politics.body:
A confidential report has found former Home Affairs secretary Michael Pezzullo acted recklessly in his engagement with a Liberal powerbroker, adding fresh detail to the conduct that led to his sacking in 2023. The finding suggests the behaviour went beyond poor judgment into territory investigators considered professionally indefensible.

Pezzullo was dismissed after text messages revealed he had discussed political matters with Liberal operative Scott Briggs over many years. The confidential report's language - 'reckless' rather than merely inappropriate - sharpens the picture of how seriously investigators viewed the relationship between one of Australia's most powerful public servants and a party insider.

sources.title: Confidential report found former home affairs boss Michael Pezzullo was ‘reckless’ in engagement with Liberal powerbroker

au-politics.headline: Major Drug Bust: Nine Charged Over 'Mother Ship' Cocaine Plot
au-politics.body:
Nine people face charges over an alleged conspiracy to import tonnes of cocaine and methamphetamine into Australia via a vessel operating as a so-called mother ship in Australian waters. The operation unravelled after a trawler sank, alerting police to the alleged smuggling network.

Victoria Police made arrests as part of the investigation, with authorities describing the syndicate as a serious organised crime operation. The scale of the alleged importation - measured in tonnes - places it among the more significant drug interceptions in recent years. Charges follow what appears to have been a lengthy covert investigation.

sources.title: Nine charged over alleged conspiracy to import tonnes of cocaine and meth via ‘mother ship’ in Australian waters
Sunken trawler alerts police to alleged drug-smuggling plan

au-politics.headline: Fuel Supply Fears Grip Farmers as Supermarkets Face Pressure
au-politics.body:
Australia's energy minister says fuel supplies remain strong and fewer service stations are running dry, but the reassurance has not quieted concern in the agricultural sector. Farmers are pushing supermarkets to absorb higher transport costs driven by fuel price volatility, arguing the supply chain squeeze is hitting producers hardest.

The competing signals - official confidence from government against on-the-ground anxiety from farmers - reflect a broader tension over who bears the cost when supply chains tighten. Supermarkets have not publicly committed to paying more for produce. The dispute lands at a politically sensitive moment, with cost-of-living pressure already a live issue heading into the federal election cycle.

sources.title: Fewer service stations running out of petrol and diesel as Australia’s fuel supplies remain strong, energy minister says
Supermarkets asked to pay more for produce to absorb fuel costs for farmers

au-politics.headline: Victorian Election Heats Up: Challenger Targets Premier Allan
au-politics.body:
Andrew Lethlean has confirmed he will contest Bendigo East at the next Victorian state election, directly challenging Premier Jacinta Allan in her own seat. The move signals the opposition's intent to put Allan under personal electoral pressure rather than cede the seat.

Bendigo East has been a safe Labor hold, but targeting a sitting premier's electorate is a standard tactic to force resources and attention away from marginal seats. Allan has not publicly responded to the challenge. The contest will draw national attention as a barometer of the government's standing in regional Victoria.

sources.title: Lethlean to challenge Victorian premier in Bendigo East

world.headline: US-Israeli War on Iran Enters Deadly New Phase
world.body:
Now in its 36th day, the US-Israeli military campaign against Iran is escalating sharply. Iran downed two American warplanes and launched missile strikes on residential areas in central Israel, while Israel extended its bombing campaign to bridges in Lebanon. The Strait of Hormuz remains a flashpoint, with analysts debating whether force will be used to reopen the critical shipping lane.

The human cost is mounting on all sides. Survivors of a US-Israeli strike on an Iranian bridge described scenes of terror, and rescue teams are conducting what officials called a harrowing search for a missing US crew member inside Iran. The conflict is now reshaping the wider region, pulling Lebanon deeper into the fighting.

sources.title: Iran war: What is happening on day 36 of US-Israeli attacks?
Iranian missile attack hits residential areas in central Israel
Rescue team in Iran face 'harrowing and dangerous' search for US crew member
Iran war live: Tehran downs 2 US warplanes; Israel bombs Lebanon bridges
Will force be used to reopen Strait of Hormuz?
Survivors of Iran bridge attack recall terror of US-Israeli strike

world.headline: Artemis II Crew Reaches Halfway Point to Moon
world.body:
NASA's Artemis II crew has passed the halfway mark on their journey to the Moon, releasing striking images of Earth taken from deep space. The photographs, described by the crew as spectacular, offer some of the sharpest views of Earth captured since the Apollo era.

The mission marks the first crewed lunar flyby in over 50 years and is a key step toward NASA's goal of returning astronauts to the lunar surface.

sources.title: Artemis II crew now halfway to Moon as they take 'spectacular' image of Earth
NASA unveils first images of Earth from Artemis II
NASA releases stunning first photos of Earth from Artemis II moon mission

world.headline: Trump Opens Protected Wilderness to Oil and Gas Drilling
world.body:
The Trump administration has moved to open large swaths of protected American wilderness to energy extraction, advancing the president's 'drill baby drill' agenda. The policy reverses conservation protections built up over decades and hands major concessions to the fossil fuel industry.

The move comes as Europe faces its own energy reckoning, with governments debating whether to revive nuclear power in response to supply shocks and rising prices. The contrasting approaches highlight a deepening transatlantic split on energy strategy.

sources.title: 'Drill baby drill': Trump opens wilderness to big energy
Faced with new energy shock, Europe asks if reviving nuclear is the answer

world.headline: Cuba Frees 2,000 Prisoners as US Pressure Bites
world.body:
Cuba has begun releasing more than 2,000 prisoners in a move widely seen as a response to sustained pressure from Washington. The releases represent one of the largest such actions by Havana in years and signal a potential shift in the island's calculus as US economic pressure intensifies.

Meanwhile, Viktor Orban faces a serious electoral challenge in Hungary after 16 years in power, with a credible opposition threatening to unseat him. Both stories reflect how authoritarian-leaning governments are navigating mounting domestic and international pressure.

sources.title: Cuba begins releasing more than 2,000 prisoners as US pressure mounts
Cuba releases over 2,000 prisoners amid mounting US pressure
After 16 years in power, could Viktor Orban finally be unseated?

ai.headline: Anthropic's Busy Week: Biotech Deal, PAC, and Claude Restrictions
ai.body:
Anthropic is moving fast on multiple fronts. The company reportedly acquired biotech startup Coefficient Bio for $400 million, signaling a push into life sciences, while simultaneously launching a political action committee to expand its influence in Washington. New research from Anthropic also probed how emotion-like concepts function internally within Claude.

On the product side, Anthropic drew sharp criticism by blocking Claude Code subscribers from using OpenClaw, a popular third-party client, unless they pay extra. The move effectively prices out a chunk of its developer base. OpenClaw itself faces separate scrutiny after a security review suggested users should assume their data may be compromised. Private market investors appear unfazed for now, though TechCrunch notes a looming SpaceX IPO could compete for the same capital.

sources.title: Emotion concepts and their function in a large language model
Anthropic is having a moment in the private markets; SpaceX could spoil the party
Anthropic essentially bans OpenClaw from Claude by making subscribers pay extra
Tell HN: Anthropic no longer allowing Claude Code subscriptions to use OpenClaw
OpenClaw gives users yet another reason to be freaked out about security
Anthropic buys biotech startup Coefficient Bio in $400M deal: Reports
Anthropic ramps up its political activities with a new PAC

ai.headline: OpenAI Reshuffles Leadership as Key Executives Step Back
ai.body:
OpenAI is reorganizing at the top. COO Brad Lightcap is moving into a new "special projects" role, while Fidji Simo, who joined to lead the applications division, is taking medical leave. The company's AGI lead is also stepping back. Three senior departures or role changes in a short window raises questions about internal stability.

The reshuffle comes as OpenAI faces intensifying competition and pressure to deliver on its commercial roadmap. No successors for the vacated roles have been publicly named.

sources.title: OpenAI executive shuffle includes new role for COO Brad Lightcap to lead ‘special projects’
OpenAI’s AGI boss is taking a leave of absence
OpenAI’s Fidji Simo Is Taking Medical Leave Amid an Executive Shake-Up

ai.headline: Musk Ties SpaceX IPO Banks to Mandatory Grok Subscriptions
ai.body:
Elon Musk is reportedly requiring banks involved in the SpaceX IPO to purchase Grok subscriptions as a condition of working on the deal. The demand blurs the line between his AI venture xAI and his aerospace company, and puts banks in an uncomfortable position - pay up or risk losing a marquee mandate.

Meanwhile, Trump's proposed NASA budget cuts land just as Artemis II astronauts orbit the Moon, capturing striking images of Earth. The administration is also facing criticism that its own tariffs are undermining the AI data center buildout it has championed, by raising costs on the hardware and energy infrastructure needed to build them.

sources.title: Trump proposes steep cut to NASA budget as astronauts head for the Moon
As Artemis II zooms to the Moon, everything seems to be going swimmingly
Elon Musk insists banks working on SpaceX IPO must buy Grok subscriptions
Trump ignores biggest reasons his AI data center buildout is failing
Artemis II crew take “spectacular” image of Earth

ai.headline: AI Erodes Critical Thinking, Meta Pauses Work After Data Breach
ai.body:
New research finds that heavy AI use is producing what scientists call "cognitive surrender" - users increasingly defer to model outputs rather than applying their own reasoning, even when the model is wrong. The finding adds weight to concerns that AI tools are reshaping how people think, not just what they produce.

Separately, Meta has paused its work with AI hiring platform Mercor after a data breach exposed sensitive industry information. The incident highlights how third-party AI vendors handling proprietary data have become a significant security liability for major tech companies.

sources.title: Meta Pauses Work With Mercor After Data Breach Puts AI Industry Secrets at Risk
"Cognitive surrender" leads AI users to abandon logical thinking, research finds

btc.headline: Bitcoin Consolidates as ETF Dominance Over Gold Looms
btc.body:
Bitcoin is grinding sideways, and analysts say that directionless action often precedes a sharp move in either direction. The longer price compresses without a catalyst, the more violent the eventual breakout tends to be.

Meanwhile, the longer-term bull case keeps building. Analyst James Seyffart argues Bitcoin ETFs will eventually surpass gold ETFs in size, pointing to Bitcoin's expanding use cases beyond a simple store of value. Gold ETFs took decades to reach their current scale. Bitcoin ETFs are moving faster.

The quantum computing threat adds a wrinkle. A widely circulated claim that quantum computers could crack Bitcoin in nine minutes is misleading - current machines lack the qubit stability and scale required. Researchers say a genuine threat remains years away, and the network has time to adapt.

sources.title: Bitcoin’s ‘no direction’ action may lead to heavier breakout: Analyst
Bitcoin ETFs 'will be larger' than gold ETFs: Analyst
Here's what 'cracking' bitcoin in 9 minutes by quantum computers actually means

btc.headline: Tether Eyes $500B Valuation but May Blink First
btc.body:
Tether is exploring a fundraising round that would value the stablecoin giant at $500 billion, but the company may shelve the effort if investor appetite falls short of that figure. That is an enormous number for a firm whose core product is a dollar-pegged token.

The hesitation signals that Tether is unwilling to accept a lower valuation and risk anchoring its price at a discount. It also reflects broader uncertainty in private markets, where high-profile crypto firms have struggled to command peak valuations amid shifting macro conditions. Tether remains one of the most profitable companies in crypto by revenue, but translating that into a public or private market price at this scale is a different challenge entirely.

sources.title: Tether may delay fundraising if demand falls short at $500B valuation: Report

btc.headline: Ethereum Foundation Hits Staking Milestone; Cambodia Targets Scammers
btc.body:
The Ethereum Foundation is closing in on its 70,000 staked ETH target, a move that signals stronger institutional commitment to the network's proof-of-stake model. Staking that volume also generates meaningful yield, giving the Foundation a more sustainable revenue stream beyond asset sales.

On the regulatory front, Cambodia is pushing legislation that would impose severe prison sentences on crypto scammers. The proposal reflects a regional pattern of governments responding to fraud with hard criminal penalties rather than financial fines. Cambodia has been a known hub for crypto-related scam operations, including pig-butchering schemes, making the legislative push both overdue and politically pointed.

sources.title: Ethereum Foundation nearly reaches 70,000 staked ETH goal
Cambodian lawmakers propose severe prison time for crypto scammers

food.name: Miso-Glazed Leek and Walnut Tart
food.description: A buttery shortcrust tart filled with slow-roasted leeks, toasted walnuts, and a white miso custard that caramelises at the edges.
food.tip: Blind-bake the shell until fully golden before adding the filling — miso custard is wetter than it looks and will make the base soggy if you skip this step.

exercise.name: The April Ladder
exercise.equipment: A set of stairs or a single step, bodyweight only
exercise.description: A stair-based interval session where you climb continuously for one minute, rest for 30 seconds, then add one extra flight or step-up variation each round, building cardiovascular load and single-leg strength progressively until you hit ten rounds.

learn.description: A trophic cascade is the dramatic, often counterintuitive chain reaction that ripples through an entire ecosystem when a single predator is added or removed — the reintroduction of wolves to Yellowstone in 1995 famously changed the course of rivers by altering where elk grazed.
learn.why: It's a masterclass in second-order thinking and reveals how ecosystems — and by extension complex systems generally — are governed by leverage points that look nothing like their effects, making it genuinely useful mental furniture beyond ecology.
